ØWow, you should like, write a book or something.Æ ØIím sorry are you mocking me? Youíre literally crying. Crying people donít get to mock.Æ That just makes Fabian laugh. ØYouíre right,Æ wiping more of the tears away. ØIím sorry.Æ And then, biting his lower lip. ØThank you. Really. Thatîjust thank you.Æ Mary gives him a small smile, hoping it looks genuine. Not because she doesnít mean it, but because she knows sheís bad at showing it. ØSure,Æ she starts to turn back to the register, ØBut just so weíre clear, if you sigh one more time like some heartbroken damsel stuck in a tower I will pour hot coffee on you.Æ That gets a proper laugh out of him. Finally. And Mary finds that she is slightly less irritated than usual when a few minutes later he starts talking her ear off about some meaningless interaction he had at the grocery store. May all my pain turn into healing so the women who come after me donít have to carry it When Mary was littleîfive? six?îshe decided that she wanted to rule the world. Regrettably, her first grade teacher informed her that Øruler of the worldÆ is, in fact, not an actual career path. Though Mary wasnít entirely convinced, she decided to settle for Prime Minister instead. And for whatever reason, that dream has stuck. Unfortunately, that means a lot of polisci courses. Now, Mary doesnít like to stereotype, but on the whole, she would say a good chunk of the people majoring in political science are white men. Often rich. Often loud. Often with annoying opinions they feel the need to share on a near constant basis. Whoever thought that Øparticipation marksÆ were a good idea clearly never had to sit in a class with someone named Braiden. Or Hayden. Or Chad. People who absolutely do not need another incentive to raise their hand and go on some boring diatribe about a lukewarm take everyone has already heard before. The other problem with polisci classes, is that Maryís very existence seems to provide ammunition for discussions that no one, including the profs, are able to resist. ØOkay, so as you have all gathered from the readings Iím sure you did,Æ the professor looks out at the class exaggeratedly, a ripple of chuckles rumbling through the lecture hall. ØTodayís topic is the secularism law, commonly known as Bill 21.Æ Thereís a powerpoint. Of course. The professor flicks through slides that describe how this bill was organized, campaigned for, and eventually passed. Itís a bill that prevents public school teachers, police officers, judges, and government lawyers, along with other civil servants in positions of authority, from wearing religious symbols at work. ØCan anyone give me an example of a religious symbol banned by the bill?Æ the prof asks, looking out at the classroom. Thereís a pause, no one moving ØOh come on, at least one of you must have read the articles?Æ Mary did read the articles, however, her arms remain firmly crossed over her chest. ØLets go people,Æ the prof continues. ØWake up. Ahîyesîyou,Æ she points to a girl sitting in the middle of the classroom. ØTurbans,Æ she says. ØCorrect! Anyone else? Another example? Yup,Æ a different student. ØCrucifixes.Æ ØMhm, any others? Yeah!Æ ØHijabs.Æ Mary feels more than a few peopleís eyes dart towards her. She does not, however, give them a reaction. She knew what this class was going to be like. She considered skipping but then, thatís never really been her style. The professor goes on to explain how studies have shown that while several religious communities have been greatly impacted by the bill, the effects have been felt most acutely by Muslim women. More staring ensues. Excellent. The professor then decides to read out a statement made by one of the researchers: ØWe saw severe social stigmatization of Muslim women,Æ the quote begins. ØAnd very disturbing declines in their sense of well-being, their ability to fulfil their aspirations, sense of safety, but also hope for the future.Æ Well. Thatís certainly depressing. ØSo I suppose itís worth asking,Æ the prof goes on, Mary already internally cringing at the discussion that is inevitably coming. ØIs secularization more harmful than helpful?Æ The hand that raises is, shockingly, not attached to a Braiden. Or a Hayden. Or a Chad. But an Ashley. This does not, surprisingly, make Mary feel any better. Ashley has blond hair, expensive looking clothes, and gives off the air of someone who likes horses just a little too much. ØI think, in the long run, itís going to be for the best,Æ she says confidently. ØIt makes everyone equal, you know? Makes us all the same, gives us all the same chances.Æ The professor arches her brow. ØBut if thatís the case, what about the quote I just read? What about the statistics that show it is negatively impacting one group more than the others? Specifically one group of women.Æ ØBut thatís a group of women who are already being oppressed,Æ Ashley says, conviction unshaken. ØIn their homes, by their families, their husbands. Who is to say that isnít whatís causing a decline in their well-being? I think forcing women to dress a certain way, to be modest, to be obedient, goes against our culture.Æ Mary almost snorts. She always finds it interesting how those words slip in there. The ØoursÆ and ØthemsÆ, the unconscious drawing of lines between the people who look like me and the people who donít. ØI donít think weíre doing those women any favours by acting like thatís okay. So hopefully things like this bill will help to eradicate that kind of thinking. To make this society a safer place for all women.Æ Mary can see it coming a mile a way. She is, regrettably, the only visibly none-christian person in the room, the only one wearing an obvious religious article, so she isnít surprised when her professorís eyes scan the room. Landing on her. ØDoes anyone have a different point of view to offer?Æ Itís work not to roll her eyes. She supposes she should be grateful sheís being given a choice here. Not just called out by name. Somewhat redundantly, Mary raises her hand. ØI just want you to clarify,Æ she says dryly, after the prof acknowledges her with a nod. ØThe thing we stand for,Æ she canít help emphasizing the word. ØIs not forcing women to do things, right?Æ Mary can already see the slight unease on Ashleyís face. Clearly she did not realize that one of these poor, oppressed, hijabi women was sitting in this room. Awkward. ØYes,Æ Ashley says finally. ØI think women should be able to wear what they want.Æ ØWhat you want,Æ Mary clarifies. And then: ØOr what the government wants, I guess. Though the two of you seem to be in agreement so, thatís nice for you.Æ Her smile is pointy. The girl blinks back at her. ØSorry?Æ ØWell, you think that Iím being forced to dress and act a certain way, so your solution is to force me to dress and act another way, right? A way that makes you feel more comfortable.Æ ØNoîI meanînot force,Æ she says, like Mary is being purposefully obtuse. ØJust, have the option to choose.Æ ØMm,Æ Mary hums sympathetically. ØSorry, my bad. Itís just that, making it a law feels a little forced to me.Æ ØOkay, I guess, yeah. But I just think, I mean, Iím not trying to be offensive,Æ Of course not. ØBut I think there is some merit to banning things that go against our core values as a society. Things that can potentially hurt people.Æ ØSure,Æ Mary says. ØIím still not clear what those core values are though? Because itís not freedom, or choice, as weíve just established. Since youíre happy to take those things away from the people you feel arenít using them correctly.Æ ØThatís not what I said,Æ clearly getting frustrated. ØIím sorry. What did you say?Æ ØI said,Æ she sighs, hand running through her long blond hair. Her friends have now started glaring at Mary. ØI said I believe in equality and I think this bill is a good step towards having a more equal society.Æ Mary just stares at her. ØRight,Æ flatly. ØExcept that, as per the articles we read for class, itís clear that is not what this bill is doing. Thereís data. Itís shown.Æ ØI donít think that the responses from one survey are enough to write the whole bill off or even to prove that thatís whatís causing these women to feel this way. We need more information, and to give the bill more time. It might actually help them.Æ Once again, Mary stares at her. Then, after a few seconds, she leans forward, lacing her hands together on top of her desk. ØDo you want to know what I think?Æ ØSure,Æ though Ashley does not at all sound like she means that. ØI think, personally, that it is a methodological mistake to base your argument on generalizations about what a diverse group of people who come from wildly different contexts may or may not want or need without having ever talked with any of them. I think that youíre picking and choosing what you want to see as sexist. For example, youíve decided that itís sexist to force women to wear a veil, but freeing to force them not to wear one. You have decided that the oppression that I face must stem entirely from my faith, my family, my culture, because I donít make the same choices you do, and since your choices are obviously the most empowering mine must be the opposite,Æ Mary takes a breath, her voice steady, calm. ØI donít think this bill is about equality, I donít even think itís really about secularization. I think it was designed to target specific people and it has succeeded in doing so. And if you donít see a problem with that then I think you may have to ask yourself whether or not you actually care about muslim women at all.Æ The classroom is quiet for a moment. Actually, Mary thinks she hears someone in the back whistle. Like a cartoon character. ØWell,Æ the professor says finally, looking unnecessarily pleased, though Mary supposes she got the student engagement she was looking for. ØAnyone else have anything to say?Æ Mourn the many selves you have not become. Mourn so you can make space for the self you have always been. Mary doesnít have anymore classes today. She can go home. Should go home. But instead she is sitting on the ground behind the library, back to the wall, trying to wait out the frustration still prickling under her skin. She feels a whole host of unpleasant emotions swirling around in her stomach that she would really like to be done with now. Itís over. The class has ended. She just needs to let it go. She needs to let go of all the things she didnít say. And all the things she did. She would like to feel, for once, like she was able to get it all out the right way. All the things she thinks. The good and the bad. The days when her veil feels like the right decision. The days when it feels like the wrong one. But she doesnít think sheís allowed that complexity. Doesnít think sheís allowed to be unsure or critical. Because they wonít really listen. Theyíll just hear what they want to. Not that they donít do that anyway. Them. We. Ours. ØWell look who it is.Æ Mary opens her eyes, looking up to find the towering figure of Sirius black leaning against the wall beside her, hair down to his shoulders, wearing a leather jacket and a pair of dark sunglasses. ØHiding?Æ he asks, sitting down next to her. Which is surprising, since the ground is damp and cold and not at all comfortable at the moment. ØNo,Æ Mary says stubbornly. Even though maybe she is. She canít really tell. After a few seconds she tilts her head back against the wall behind her, looking up at the overcast sky. Sirius laughs. ØUh-huh, just here for the scenery then?Æ Gesturing to the parking lot in front of them. Mary doesnít bother answering and after a decent stretch of silence Sirius wags a cigarette at her. ØYou mind?Æ he asks. She shakes her head, watching him light it between his lips and inhale. He moans. ØFuck, that feels good.Æ Mary arches her brow. ØYou trying to cut back?Æ ØHell no,Æ Sirius exhales, smoke disappearing in the air in front of him. ØJames is being a dick about it though. Now that weíre getting close to the championships heís on a whole health kick.Æ Sirius shudders, like itís the worst thing he can imagine. ØItís like heís an athlete or something,Æ Mary says dryly. ØHar, har, har.Æ The silence returns, itís something the pair of them have always been surprisingly good at. Mary doesnít actually think her and Sirius have that much in common but for some reason they can read one another so well. Thereís never any awkwardness. Any uncertainty. They can go weeks without talking and one look at his face will tell her everything she needs to know. ØSo,Æ Sirius says eventually. Mary rolls her head towards him, waiting for him to continue because she has no intention of helping him out. ØYou know, your turn,Æ he gestures with his cigarette. ØI told you why Iím back here freezing my ass off. Whatís up with you?Æ Mary goes back to looking at the bleak February sky. ØTold you. No reason.Æ ØOh come on,Æ Sirius gives her a nudge with his elbow. ØTell me.Æ ØYou just want gossip.Æ Siriusís grin is razor sharp. ØOf course.Æ ØSorry to disappoint, but itís not that interesting.Æ ØTell me anyway.Æ Mary pauses, flipping through her thoughts like records in a milk carton, trying to pick out the one she actually wants to listen to. ØI had aèdiscussion in class.Æ ØOh yeah?Æ itís to his credit that he doesnít sound bored, dark eyes watching her intently, waiting for her to continue. ØWhat about, exactly?Æ She almost smirks. ØMe.Æ Thereís a beat, she wonders if sheíll have to explain but then: ØYou need me to beat someone up?Æ Stupid. Sweet. But stupid. Sheís gives him a flat look. ØI can fight my own battles.Æ ØMary.Æ ØSirius.Æ ØDid someone say some racist shit?Æ he goes on when she doesnít. ØBecause I swear to god Iíll fuck them up.Æ ØThatíll get you kicked off the team,Æ she says flatly. ØWorth it.Æ ØJames wonít be happy.Æ ØHeíll live.Æ Mary rolls her eyes. ØRelax Prince Charming, it wasnít like that. It wasnít anything really, justèÆ she trails off, she doesnít even know what it was, Øexhausting,Æ she decides on finally. When Sirius doesnít say anything to that she sighs. ØThe thing isîØ she doesnít know how to say this. ØThe thing I actually canít stand. Is being put in a box.Æ Sirius arches his brow. ØA box?Æ ØYes. Itís like thereís a good box and a bad box and you have to choose where to put everything and I just donít think any of it works like that. I donít think anything is all good or all bad. Itís likeîthereís parts of Islam I agree with, and parts I donít. And I feel like everyone else gets to have that, you know? Like if you wear a cross around your neck no one assumes you agree with every word of the bible, or the fucking crusades, but me?Æ she shakes her head, looking angrily at the ground. ØThereís shitty people in every church, shitty people praying to every god, in every country, wearing every kind of clothing. But I feel like Iím not allowed to say that. ØThe Braidens, and Haydens, and Chads, and Ashleys.Æ ØAh.Æ ØAnd it just makes me want to punch someone sometimes.Æ Sirius doesnít speak right away, taking a slow drag from his cigarette while Mary catches her breath. Sheís not usually this chatty, and she can feel her capacity for dealing with other people quickly dwindling. ØI get that,Æ Sirius says finally. Mary huffs. ØDo you?Æ He nods, wrist resting on his knee, smoke between his fingers. ØSure. Or, well, I donít get it but I agree. My motherís a real cunt and sheís Catholic so,Æ he looks at her. ØShitty people in every church, right?Æ Mary likes him so much she almost smiles. ØRight.Æ Another pause, before; ØMy momís kind of amazing.Æ ØYeah?Æ Sirius asks. She nods, not quite looking at him. ØSheís justèreally good at the mom thing, you know?Æ ØThatís cool.Æ ØYeah.Æ Memories of birthday parties, of family gatherings, of Ramadan nights. Everyone staying up long after the sun. Breaking their fast. You could taste the love in those meals sheíd swear it. ØDíyou think sheíd mind, that you, yíknow, donít wanna put it all in the good box?Æ Mary thinks about it, even though, in the end, she doesnít really need to. ØNo,Æ laughing a little and kicking at the ground. ØShe was actually surprised when I told her I wanted to wear a hijab.Æ ØOh yeah?Æ Sirius smiles at her. ØIíve never been very good at following rules I guess. ØShe wanted it to be my choice. And it was. It is.Æ Itís cold, too cold for them to be sitting outside on the ground, but neither of them try to move. The snow has definitely soaked through Maryís pants at this point, the sun fading fast from the sky even though itís not even five yet. To divide a room just my stepping into it. ØMy mother wears one,Æ Mary says finally. ØAnd donít you forget it.Æ Everybody needs somebody. Behind the moon there is a sun. Itís early evening when she gets home, the sun outside already set, the sky a deep blue. The apartment is mostly dark, Marlene and Dorcas clearly not home, a warm orange-yellow glow slipping beneath the door of Lilyís room, the low hum of music tapping on the walls. Mary stands outside, having a staring contest with the doorknob. Part of her is too tired, the day has warn her out, she needs to recharge and sheís never been the type to do that with other people. Preferring to disappear into her cave. Quiet, dark, alone. No one is stopping her from doing that now. Except. ØCome in!Æ Lily sings when Mary finally knocks, pushing her way inside. Lilyís arm seems to come around her automatically, a quiet laugh escaping her. The noise runs along the top of Maryís skin like fingertips. ØYou okay?Æ Mary suppresses a yawn. Fuck itís only seven, what is she, ninety? ØLong day,Æ she finally mutters. Lily hums, bending down to press a kiss to the top of her head before she goes on typing on her laptop. Mary closes her eyes for a bit, not sleeping so much as drifting. Floating. ØWell, youíre always a dick so, Iím kinda used to it.Æ Mary snorts, but Lily doesnít start typing again. Though, eventually, her eyes drift closed again. ØIíve never asked you about your family,Æ Lily says finally. Mary isnít sure what to say to that so she doesnít say anything, and eventually Lily goes on. ØI just kind of assumed, I mean, because youíre you.Æ ØIím me?Æ Mary mumbles. ØYeah,Æ Lily lets out one of those little laughs again, and then, like she canít help it, kisses Maryís temple. ØYouíreèupfront. Fearless.Æ ØFearless? Really, Evans?Æ ØWell you are!Æ Lily gives her a squish. ØBravest person I know.Æ Mary doesnít dignify that with an answer, at least not a real one, just making a vague sort of noise. So. ØNo,Æ she forces herself to say, finally. ØThey donít know.Æ Lily hums, hand making comforting circles along Maryís back. ØWould it be a problem? Is itèlikeènot allowed orè?Æ Mary snorts, even though itís not really funny. ØNot allowed,Æ mockingly. ØWell!Æ Mary laughs again, burrowing into Lily a little more. She doesnít answer right away. Doesnít know exactly how to answer. There are layers to the reasons that her mother might reject her. A spiderweb of expectations she is failing to meet. ØI guess it depends,Æ Mary finally says. And I knew it would understand me. Chapter End Notes Hopefully more soon! His therapist, in one of their many sessions, which accomplished nothing as far as Regulus can tell, asked him about the first time he remembers thinking about food. And heís pretty sure he was eight. He has this memory that he isn't confident is real, of sitting in the gymnasium at lunch, staring at his sandwich and thinking I canít eat this. Spiders climbing up the insides of his skin. I canít eat this. I canít eat this. I donít deserve it. I hate myself. He didnít admit that last bit to the therapist, so maybe the reason those sessions didnít accomplish anything is him. Therapy, he suspects, requires a certain amount of honesty to work, and Regulus has never been very good at honesty. He lies without meaning to, they just come out of him. A defence mechanism, he imagines, born from years of tiptoeing around his mother. I hate myself. is something Regulus thinks a lot. Sometimes he doesnít even realize heís doing it. Heíll zone outîwalking to class, folding his clothes, cleaning his roomîand suddenly itíll hit him, that heís just been listening to the same thought running on a loop through his head over and over again. I hate myself. I hate myself. I hate myself. Itís not exactly original is it? Not very eloquent. Itís almost childish. I hate myself. I hate myself. I hate myself. If you were to ask Regulus why he doesnít eat much. Or sleep much. Or talk much. He could give you a dozen different answers. Iím busy. It slows me down. It doesnít feel right. But at the end of the day, the root of the problem is always the same. I hate myself. I hate myself. I hate myself. Heís stopped going to classes. He tells himself itís because itís a waste of time, it makes more sense to work on the actual assignments than to sit in a room watching a powerpoint he can easily access on his own. Heís just being efficient. He tries not to dwell on the fact that the idea of stepping into a lecture hall full of people has started to make claws drag across his skin. The idea of being seen, of being called on, making a sick, heavy feeling grow in the pit of his stomach. Heís stopped going to work as well. Too embarrassed, after being found on the floor by Severus. Too shaky, to bear facing Lucius. Or Nicolai. He just wants to hide. Just wants to shrink. His life wasnít that big to begin with but as the weeks of the semester drag on he makes it smaller and smaller. Goes out of his way to avoid people. He doesnít sleep much, spends most of his time at the library. Heís there when it opens. There when it closes. People ignore him, theyíre quiet, they keep to themselves. Regulus likes that. He can handle that. He spends all his time working on his school assignments but heís not certain he actually manages to get anything done. His mind is fuzzy. It jumps all over the place, unable to focus properly on one thing. Sirius texts him but he ignores it. His mother calls but he ignores that too. He tries not to cut James off completely but he knows he isnít doing a good job. Giving him short, one word answers. Avoiding his requests for Regulus to come over. He reads books but absorbs nothing. Writes pages and pages of essays he knows arenít any good. He just needs to get to the end of the semester and thenîand thenî Well. He doesnít know whatíll happen exactly. Something good maybe? Something better than this. The people who know where to find him drift in and out of the seat across from him. It is work for him not to apologize to them. Iím sorry you have to put up with this. Iím sorry Iím never able to be there when you need me. Iím sorry I need you so much more. I hate myself. I hate myself. I hate myself. Øîand so of course Xeno made about three dozen of them,Æ Cerci snorts, beside him. Regulus blinks, trying to focus on the conversation. ØThree dozen what?Æ ØApple and artichoke cookies!Æ Reg squints. ØAnd artichoke?Æ His stomach lurches at the thought. Cerci smiles. ØYup, they were likeèscaly almost? Like little dragon turds!Æ She laughs, and Regulus manages a wane smile. ØDid Evan eat one just to be polite?Æ he canít remember if she mentioned Evan being there, but since heís over at Cerciís more often than not these days, he assumes itís a safe bet. Her smile grows bigger. ØHe ate two.Æ Regulus pulls a disgusted face, making Cerci laugh again. Heís only met Cerciís roommate once or twice, but each time heís found Xeno to be far too much. Heís not even sure what theyíre too much of. Just. Everything. Their clothes too loud, their voice too cheery, always saying things that make no sense to Regulus. And whatever Xeno is doing in the kitchen is nothing that Regulus wants any part of. ØEvan is too nice,Æ Regulus says finally. But Cerci is still smiling. ØI donít think so,Æ the braids on either side of her head swinging when she shakes it. ØI think heís perfect.Æ Regulus makes a gagging noise and Cerci kicks him under the table. ØDonít be a dick,Æ she hisses, though she doesnít really sound upset. ØDonít be disgusting then.Æ So, of course, she sticks her tongue out at him. At some point Cerci leaves. Regulus doesnít really know when. The table heís at isnít near any windows and he keeps forgetting to check the time. It passes. That heís fairly certain of. Though it's not just the lack of natural sunlight that's made time hard to keep track of recently. Regulus has also started falling asleep periodically, waking up with his face smushed into his keyboard. Itíll happen so fast he wonít even notice. And it makes everythingèblurry. ØYou promised.Æ His brother is standing next to him. Heís dressed in his hockey gear, helmet in his hand, stern look on his face. ØYou promised you would tell me if you needed help.Æ Regulus just blinks at him. ØWhat are you doing here?Æ ØYou promised Reg.Æ He swallows, his throat tight. ØI know,Æ he croaks. And then: ØI donít need help.Æ ØLiar.Æ ØI donít, I justîI just need time. Thatís all. Iíll figure this out. Iím figuring it out.Æ ØYouíve had time.Æ ØI just need a little more.Æ ØItís getting like it was last time.Æ And suddenly Regulusís fear is replaced with something more biting. ØHow would you know what it was like last time? You werenít even there.Æ Siriusís face twists, and thenîand then heís screaming. Regulusís heart jumps in his chest, Siriusís hands clutching his head as he starts sinking to his knees. ØSirius?Æ Heís on his back. Heís on the ice. Heís in the library. ØSirius?Æ Thereís blood around his head. He went down and he didnít get up. Not for a while. For too long. He hit his head. ØSirius!Æ But that was months ago. That wasîthat wasîheís on the ice. It was just a nightmare. This is why he doesnít sleep. ØI know itís bad luck and everything,Æ Evan is saying, several hours later. Or maybe itís the next day? Regulus isnít actually sure. What he is sure of, is that Evan never studies when he comes to the library. He brings books, but never looks at them. How he isnít failing Regulus has no idea. ØBut I think James and Sirius are starting to get to me because I really think we could win this thing.Æ Regulus glances up at him and then away. ØYouíve been playing well.Æ Evan nods. ØYeah, the team has reallyîØ ØNot the team,Æ Regulus interrupts. ØYou. Youíve been playing well. ØWow, thatís kind of like our thing, huh?Æ Someone walks by their table, shooting them an irritated look as they go. Probably because Evan has never heard of an inside voice. He says everything like heís yelling it across a hockey rink. Regulus has tried to train him to whisper but itís no use. No amount of positive reinforcement seems able to dim him. ØWhatís our thing?Æ Regulus grumbles, bending a little further over his work. Heís not sure why people looking at him is making his skin crawl lately but it is. ØDude.Æ Regulus stares at Evan in a manner that he hopes communicates how ridiculous he thinks that is. ØDude is not our thing. I donít even know what having a ØthingÆ means.Æ ØThen how do you know our thing isnít dude?Æ Evan is enjoying this far too much. He always does. He likes to tease Regulus and Regulus lets him because he likes making Evan smile, even if it isnít always on purpose. Regulus just shakes his head. ØYouíre being ridiculous.Æ ØWhatever dude.Æ Regulus groans, Evan laughing loud enough that Regulus expects the whole campus hears him. Several angry ØshushesÆ sent their way. ØYou need to come home,Æ his motherís nails dig into his shoulder. For a second heís back standing on the lawn the day he left for school. Her mouth close to his ear: donít let me down. A shiver runs through him, the fluorescent lights flickering over his head. Heís back in the library, his mother still standing behind him. ØI never should have let you leave, this whole thing was ridiculous from the start.Æ Regulus grits his teeth. Hands curled into fists on the table top. ØI donít want to come home.Æ She scoffs. ØWell, I think itís clear that acting according to what you want is hardly the most prudent course of action.Æ ØIíll get my grades up.Æ A cold laugh. ØWill you? When? How?Æ ØIíve asked for extensions. Iím workingîØ ØEnough Regulus,Æ another squeeze of his shoulder. ØYour excuses are pointless and boring. Youíre coming home.Æ ØNo.Æ ØYou arenít ready to be on your own. And I was a fool to think that Sirius would bother to look after you. ØWhat?Æ ØAnswer the phone.Æ Which is when he hears the ringing. His eyes blink open to find his motherís name flashing on the screen of his phone. He pulls his head off the table and watches it. He doesnít pick it up. He knows what sheís going to say. He doesnít think Severus comes looking for him, but for all their differences, they have similar habits. So he isnít entirely shocked to see him one day, an old book in his hands and an irritated look on his face. Regulus is slightly more surprised when Severus stops in front of him. He considers ignoring him, but, well, since the last time Regulus saw Severus he was peeling him off the bathroom floor, he supposes he deserves better than that. ØHi?Æ Regulus says finally, when Severus remains silent. The other boyís face doesnít soften. ØYouíve stopped coming to work.Æ Regulus grimaces. He doesnít really feel like having this conversation, doesnít really know how to have it. ØIím sick.Æ Severus looks him up and down. ØClearly.Æ Regulusís eyes dart around them, the last thing he needs is for Evan or Cerci to show up right now. Heís managed to keep the information that heís giving-up on his internship a secret from them so far. Heís been able to keep most things a secret from them, honestly. Heís never sure if the lying is a symptom or a disease itself. But once you start itís hard to stop. Once you start itís hard not to feel like you have to keep everything hidden. Itís addictive in a way. Like maybe itís possible, to make people see you exactly how you want them to. ØYou know people would kill for that job,Æ Severus goes on. ØAnd youíre just, what? Itís not going to anyone. You took it. Thatís someoneís chance gone now. Wasted.Æ Regulus just keeps staring at his book, knee bouncing up and down. ØIím sorry,Æ he manages finally. Itís not an easy word to say. Not for him. Not to Snape of all people. He keeps waiting for the older boy to walk away but he doesnít. Casting a shadow across Regulusís table. ØWhy?Æ he asks finally. And Regulus canít help it, he looks up. ØWhy?Æ Severus waves his hand impatiently. ØYes, why now? Why canít you do it now? Youíve put up with Nicolaiís bullshit all year,Æ and then, almost begrudgingly. ØYouíve put up with my bullshit. Why canít you do it now? Why not finish?Æ Regulus doesnít know how to answer, nor does he appreciate the pressure building behind his ribs. Or his eyes. The scratchiness in his throat. Why? Because he feels as though this year has slowly been peeling the layers of his skin off and heís gotten so close to the bone now that everything hurts. Everything takes too much. Makes him feel too much. He canít defend against it. His shields are gone. Thereís is nothing left to stop the world from getting in. ØI justèÆ his voice breaks, embarrassing. ØI canít.Æ He expects Severus to flay him for that. Expects him to scoff in that haughty, patronizing way he does and walk away. But he doesnít. His dark eyes narrow, head tilting to the side. And then he says something that Regulus was not at all expecting: ØWhat happened with Lucius?Æ Regulus feels like he might be sick. ØWhat?Ø his voice isnít very brave at the moment, barely making it out of his mouth as his nails scratch at the surface of the table. Eyes darting around again. He doesnít know who he thinks is listening. Who he thinks would care. But he canít get rid of this feeling of being seen. ØHe couldnít stop singing your praises those first few weeks, and then, one dayèÆ Severus snaps his fingers, making Regulus flinch. HR or?Æ Finally, Regulus manages to work some moisture back into his mouth, enough to speak. Hands still fidgeting. Leg still bouncing. He is too raw for this. Heís been skinned. ØNo.Æ Severus keeps looking at him. Seeing too much. Everyone always sees too much. He wishes they would cover their eyes. Heís such an ugly pathetic thing. I hate myself. I hate myself. I hate myself. ØWhy?Æ It almost makes him laugh. ØBecause,Æ he manages, shaking his head, looking down. ØWho would have believed me? Who would have cared?Æ Thereís silence, the distant sound of footsteps down one of the aisles. A door opening. A chair being pushed back from a desk. Severus is looking for a way to explain Regulusís unravelling but there isnít one. With the same vague irritation he seems to say everything. But when Regulus looks into his eyes he thinks he might actually mean it. ØIíll believe you,Æ the other boy repeats, like heís worried that maybe Regulus didnít hear him the first time. Regulus opens his mouth and then closes it. Not sure what to say. Nails scratching, scratching, scratching. He wasnít prepared for this conversation. He thought he was done thinking about Lucius. It hadnít been important. He knows that. It didnít even effect him that much. Heís pretty sure. He thinks. ØIt doesnít matter Severus.Æ ØTell me anyway.Æ ØIîØ canít, canít, canít. Regulus scrubs at his face. Itís hard to sit still, everything in him pulling apart. God, heís fucked. Heís so fucked. ØIt was justîhe justîhe touched me a lot, those first few weeks. Texted me. HeîØ Regulus grimaces. ØOne day he started massaging my shoulders and James walked in. He made it a whole thing. It wasnít really. It wasnít anything really.Æ Silence. Regulus isnít looking at him. Heís trying to breathe. His lungs seem to have shrunk in size over the last few minutes. His cheeks are hot, flushed with embarrassment. With guilt. ØPotter punch him?Æ Severus asks finally. Regulus lets out a startled laugh. ØNo,Æ he says. ØBut he did choke him a bit.Æ Severus nods, eyes intense. ØGood.Æ Regulus isnít entirely sure what day it is. He has some vague idea that it might be a Wednesday? Or Thursday? Some middling day that doesnít really matter. He gets to the library before Evan even wakes up, the sky outside dark. Heís standing in the cold, bouncing back and forth between his feet, trying to keep warm, when they unlock the doors. No one else is there yet. And the calm that brings him is a welcomed relief. He climbs to the top floor, maneuvering his way to a table pushed back into a dark corner. Barely visible. He just needs to hand in one assignment. He just needs to pass. He just needs to survive this semester and then, and then, and then. And then. and then. andthenandthenandthenandthen and then and then and then and then ad n h n te n a d e t h n a d n t e n h . Eventually things have to get better, right? Isnít that what everyone is always saying? His exams are coming up soon. He doesnít go to class but he can read a syllabus. He doesnít know how heís going to manage that. How heíll be able to force himself to walk into a crowded gymnasium filled with hundreds of students. Mostly he tries not to think about it at all. He could probably email his professors, ask for accommodations, ask for help. But the thought makes his skin crawl almost as much as the thought of the exams themselves. The shame burning holes deep in his stomach. So instead he just keeps his head down and tries to make some sense of the word doc in front of him. ØHi.Æ Regulus feels his whole body go stiff. His hand hovering over the spacebar on his laptop. He doesnít lift his eyes, listening instead as the chair next to him is pulled out. Watching out of the corner of his eye as the figure beside him settles into his seat. ØSo,Æ James says, when Regulus continues to act like heís been turned to stone. ØYouíve been avoiding me.Æ Regulus would like to argue, but his brain is too muddled to come up with a convincing lie at the moment. So he just sticks with his current strategyîplaying dead. And Regulus almost laughs. ØHi,Æ he says in return. ØIíve missed you.Æ A punch to the gut. ØIíve missed you too,Æ Regulus manages to say. Though honestly heís not sure how. ØCome home with me?Æ Regulus sparks and flares and threatens to combust. He canít. He shouldnít. Heís not sure what he can handle right now, what he can deal with. This is his safe space. His routine. Heís carved out a hole for himself and itís been working so far. He needs to say no. He canít leave. He canít step outside the very dark lines he has drawn around himself. ØOkay,Æ the word comes out of him without his permission. But once again it brings a smile to Jamesís face, so Regulus canít find it in him to take it back. With shaking hands he packs away his stuff. Sliding his laptop and notes into his bag, walking side-by-side with James out of the library. They donít talk much, James waving to a few people he knows on their way to his car, Regulus keeping his head down. ØThey wonít assume thereís anything going on between us,Æ James says quietly, misunderstanding the reason for Regulusís shame. It isnít him. It isnítèthem. Itís Regulus. He doesnít want to be seen. Their eyes have hands. He feels their gazes like fingers running along his arms. Squeezing his chin. Digging into his shoulder. Regulus is grateful, it makes him feel less guilty for not talking. He doesnít know what heís doing. Doesnít know why he agreed to this. James needs to be as far away from him as possible right now. Needs to not see any of this. This has to be the end of them. It has to be. Because how could James possibly want him after this? Regulus zones out, eyes looking out the window but not taking in the scenery. When the car eventually stops heís jolted awake, sitting up and expecting to see the familiar sight of the Gryffindor Residence building in front of him. But as he blinks, forcing the world into focus, he sees that that isnít where they are at all. Beside them is the diner he once accosted James inîapologizing and embarrassingly admitting to keeping tabs on his hockey stats. James undoes his seatbelt but doesnít get out. Waiting for Regulus. This is a test. And Regulus is angry. And heís scared. And he feels betrayed. ØIím not hungry.Æ ØRegulusîÆ ØWhat are we doing here James? What is this?Æ ØItís dinner Reg. Thatís it.Æ Regulus shakes his head. ØFuck you.Æ ØRegulus, please,Æ all facades done away with. ØPlease, Iím askingîbegging. I donít care what it is, just eat something. You look sick.Æ ØFuck. You.Æ A sentiment that would be stronger if his voice wasnít wobbling quite so much. ØPlease.Æ ØWhat was this, some kind of-of set-up?Æ he sounds hysterical even to his own ears. He feels hysterical. Out of control. There are voices in his head. Under his skin. And right now they are so loud. They are telling him to run. To scream and shout and break things. They are telling him that James is the enemy and this is a war and he will not fucking lose. Not after all this time. Jamesís face is so pitiful, he really needs to learn to hide his feelings better. ØDo I need a set.up to get you to eat?Æ Regulus just keeps shaking his head. ØTake me home. I want to go home.Æ ØIs there something else we couldîØ ØYou donít get to control me like this.Æ Nails digging into his shoulder. Donít let me down. ØRegulus,Æ Jamesís voice breaks. ØYouíre not my fucking mother.Æ ØRegulus.Æ ØTake me home. I want you to take me home,Æ he says again, voice full of splinters. He is coming apart. He is being undone. Unwound. He canít deal with this. Heís too frayed. James turns in his seat, facing Regulus full-on, hands held out in front of him, pleading. ØIím scared, Iím fucking scared Reg, okay? Youíre scaring me. Just tell me what it is, whatís wrong? Why wonít youîis this some kind ofèbody image thing, or?Æ Regulus brings his hands up, covering his face. There is a fear that is so great that you canít put it into wordsîwhen the thing you have spent so much time running from finally catches up to you. The movies would have you believe that there is a sense of relief in that moment. When you finally let go, and face the thing you have to. But the movies are full of shit. It is the worst thing Regulus has ever felt in his entire life. ØJames stop. Leave it. Just. Leave it.Æ ØI canít,Æ Jamesís voice is a mess, Regulus thinks they might both be crying. ØI canít, I love you too much.Æ Fuck. Fuck this. Fuck all of this. ØIím sorry,Æ Regulus manages, a boy in the dark. A ship lost at sea. ØDonít be sorry, you donítîjust have dinner with me okay?Æ James sniffles, trying to smile. ØThatís it. Thatís all.Æ Thatís all. And he really wishes it was that simple. He does. Wishes he could just switch his brain off. Wishes he could just be normal about this. ØI canít.Æ ØRegîØ ØI canít,Æ he repeats. And he doesnít know how to say it any clearer. Itís not I donít want to itís I canít. I canít, I canít, I canít. ØIím sorry. Iím so sorry.Æ ØReg, no, hey,Æ and James reaches out, the first time since the library, pulling Regulusís hands away from his face and clutching them between his own. ØDonít be sorry.Æ But he is. God he is. ØIf I could be better for you I would,Æ Regulus says, in a moment of pathetic weakness. Jamesís face crumples. ØNo, shh, donít say that,Æ he pulls Regulus towards him and Regulus goes, face burying itself in Jamesís neck, the older boyís arms coming awkwardly around him, the gear stick still between them. ØI donít want you to be better, I donítîI just want you to be okay.Æ Regulus pushes into him as much as their current positions will allow. Breathing in the scent of him, basking in the warmth. ØI donít know how to be okay.Æ James makes a wounded noise, hand rubbing up and down Regulusís back. For a long moment they just stay like that, wrapped up in one another, sniffling, and hiccuping. Regulusís breathing has only just started to even out when James speaks again: ØI think we need to go to the hospital, Reg.Æ ØNo,Æ the sound is whiny and weak, Regulus pushing back to look at James. ØNo, please, James please donít make meîyou donít know whatîitísîI canít do that again. Please, donít make me do that again. Fuck. Iíll get better. I promise. Regulus canít tell which of them is shaking more. ØYou need to eat Regulus,Æ James whispers, and Regulus closes his eyes. ØI donít know what else to do.Æ I hate myself. I hate myself. I hate myself. He knows what will happen. Heís been here before. Hands handling him roughly, people talking over him, poking and prodding. Casually dismissing him. They say the voices in his head wonít kill him. They say the panic that rises in chest wonít stop his heart. They say he can ignore them. Just ignore them. They'll go away eventually, if you tell the therapist enough. If you take enough pills. But theyíre wrong. Theyíre wrong. And itís so fucking scary. ØPlease,Æ James is still whispering. ØPlease, for me?Æ ØThatís not fair,Æ Regulus croaks. ØI know,Æ James admits. ØBut Iím asking anyway. I need you to be okay Regulus, alright? I need you to beîI canítîØ but his voice breaks, choking him on the way out, and his words fade away. Falling between the seats. Hiding in the glove box. Heís still holding onto Jamesís wrists, so he gives them a squeeze, forcing his eyes to open. James is a mess. They both are, he imagines, infinitely grateful that he doesnít have to look at his own face right now. But he doesnít hate James. ØYeah?Æ ØBut Iím not being admitted, Iíll stay for their fucking tests or whatever, but that's it, no matter what the doctors say, alright?Æ James is nodding, he doesnít really understand. Heís normal. Heís never been through this. Doesnít realize how easily the system sucks you up. How little choice it gives you. How little choice it thinks you deserve. ØSure, okay. To start the car. ØJames,Æ Regulus says cautiously. ØThis wonít fix anything.Æ Because it wonít. Because it never does. James gives him a weak smile. ØI know,Æ and then: ØBut maybe itíll help?Æ And Regulus doesnít have the heart to correct him. They sit in a waiting room, a paper number in hand, under fluorescent lights, in uncomfortable chairs, surrounded by people in various states of distress. People who actually deserve to be there. People whose pain is out of their control. Regulus feels his shame like an anchor, making his whole body sink. The number is called. Regulus moves to another chair, this one in front of a nurse in a booth, James hovering over his shoulder. The nurse asks him questions: whatís his address? Whatís his phone number? Can she see his health card? His ID? Why is he there? ØI canítèeat,Æ which isnít the truth, but isnít a lie either. ØAnd heís not sleeping,Æ James says, Regulus would glare at him but he hasnít got the energy. The woman doesnít look up from the computer in front of her and, presumably, the form sheís filling out. ØAny fever? Vomiting?Æ ØNo fever,Æ he bites his lower lip. ØSome vomiting.Æ He can feel James looking at him, he doesnít meet his gaze. A few more standard questions are asked. Then he holds out his arm and a plastic bracelet is wrapped around his wrist and heís sent back to the waiting room. Itís not like the waiting room in a doctorís office. Thereís no music. No generic art on the walls. Thereís a TV screen where the numbers flash up when itís your turn to be processed. Regulus curls over his knees. The smell of the place makes him feel woozy. All hospitals have the same smellîcleaning product, barely masking something rotten. James reaches out, rubs his back, Regulus barely manages not to shrug him off. The shame grows. Itís nearly unbearable. Eventually theyíre called again, along with several other patients, and lead down a series of hallways into the ER proper. Thereís a desk, behind which doctors and nurses sit in front of various computers and on various phones, rushing in and out of the room. In front of the desk are beds, most surrounded by curtains that have been pulled closed. And then there are more chairs. Theyíre herded into these chairs to resume their waiting. The hospital is crawling on his skin. He feels worse just being here. Sicker. Not that he is sick. Heísèwellèheís not sick. He wraps his arms protectively around himself, not looking at anyone, especially not at James. James who should be in a diner having dinner with someone who doesnít mind being called his boyfriend. Not sitting in the ER. ØRegulus Black?Æ He grimaces, looking up at a man in scrubs. He gives Regulus a curt smile before gesturing to one of the now vacant beds. James follows him, standing next to the bed as Regulus sits on the side. ØHi Regulus,Æ the man saysîmid-thirties maybe? Brown hair, scruff on his jaw, kind eyes. ØIím Dr. Tanner. What seems to be the problem today?Æ Heís looking at a clipboard so Regulus imagines he knows what the problem is, but he clearly wants Regulus to explain himself anyway. ØIím having trouble eating,Æ he actually wants to claw the words out of the air. Wants to rinse out his mouth. Itís so fucking embarrassing. The doctor nods, putting the clipboard aside. ØCan you roll up your sleeve for me?Æ Regulus does, watching as the man wraps a familiar band around his upper arm and starts taking his blood pressure. ØWhat exactly is making it hard for you to eat?Æ he asks mildly, watching the numbers. ØIs it painful? Do you have trouble keeping it down?Æ Regulus feels naked. Horribly exposed. Itís awful, something rotting in the pit of his stomach. ØI have trouble getting the food down. But, no, itís notèpainful.Æ The man nods, taking the armband off of Regulus and writing something down. ØAre you experiencing a loss of appetite?Æ Regulus doesnít know how to answer that. ØI donít reallyèhave an appetite,Æ he responds awkwardly. ØI see,Æ Dr. Tanner says, still with the same polite voice. Gentle eyes. ØAnd is this a new problem?Æ Regulus grits his teeth. ØNo,Æ he manages. ØHow long would you say this has been going on, give or take?Æ Regulus stares blankly back at him. After several seconds he opens his mouth but then closes it again. He has years of memories of moving food around his plate. Of dreading dinner. It was the one rule he could never really followîeating his food. He was åpickyí his mother would say, with distaste. A very gauche thing to be in her opinion. But for most of his life thatís all it was. And then Sirius left. Heís never been sure if Sirius was really helping him that much with food, and thatís why it all started to fall apart without him. Or if Sirius justèhelped. If having him around just helped in every way. Regardless, his leaving really drove home how fragile Regulus is. How fragile he still is. Fuck. ØReg?Æ James asks, and Regulus blinks, looking up at him and then away. Heís taken too long to answer. A concerning amount of time judging by the faces of the two men looking at him. He clears his throat. ØI donít know how long itís been going on,Æ he manages, and then, resigned, and in a voice barely audible: ØA while.Æ James seems confused by that. The doctor less so. ØI want to get a blood test done and also have a look at you with the ultrasound, make sure thereís nothing mechanical weíre dealing with. One of the nurses will bring in a gown, just take off your shirt, your pants can stay on, and then theyíll put the IV in, okay?Æ No. No. No. Regulus nods with difficulty. Throat tight. ØOkay.Æ ØIV?Æ James asks, looking mildly concerned, his eyes flicking back and forth between Regulus and the doctor. ØIsn't that for like, surgery and stuff?Æ That makes the doctor laugh. ØNo.Æ ØIt's just gives them access to your veins,Æ Regulus supplies, still not quite able to look at James. ØTo draw blood or give you fluids. It's really not a big deal.Æ James makes an ØoÆ shape with his mouth just as the doctor says: ØVery good. What are you, pre-med?Æ Regulus can't tell if it's a joke, it's not as though someone couldn't pick up what an IV is from watching a few episodes of any medical drama on TV. He answers anyway, just incase it's not: ØNo.Æ Not bothering to explain further. He is not proud of how familiar he is with hospitals. With tubes and needles and holes in his skin. He'd rather not put himself on display any further. ØRight,Æ the doctor says after an awkward pause. ØWell, Iíll see you boys once we get some of these results back, alright?Æ James does him the curtesy of turning away when Regulus undresses. He doesnít even need to ask. There are few things as dehumanizing as a hospital gown, at least in Regulusís opinion. The IV is inserted in his arm, near the inside of his elbow, needle irritating his skin as three small, short tubes hang out of it. When they start filling vials with his blood his skin grows hot, pins and needles running through his body until dots cloud his vision. Like a television screen thatís lost its signal. When he comes to heís lying on his back on the bed, a nurse hovering over him, James on his other side looking nervous, a blood pressure band wrapped around his arm again. When he manages to speak what he says is: ØIím going to be sick.Æ He doesnít know where the nurse gets the bin from, but a second later itís there, Regulus leaning over the side of his bed and regurgitating the contents of his stomach. There isnít much. His arm hurts. His head hurts. He wants to go home. He does not get to go home. The ER is not the most efficient place on the planet. Thereís a lot of waiting. A lot of prodding and poking. James is beside him for all of it. Or, well, most of it anyway. Regulus wishes he wasnít. Wishes he wasnít here for any of this. ØAlright Regulus,Æ Dr. Tanner says as he walks back through the curtains several hours after the first time. ØBefore I start discussing this all with you,Æ he gestures with the clipboard in his hands. ØI want to make sure that youíre okay with me talking about it in front of your friend?Æ Regulus looks briefly at James and then away. He doesnít answer, hands curling into fists. He doesnít want to have to say the words out loud. After several seconds of painful silence James coughs, clearing his throat and standing up out of the uncomfortable chair heíd been sitting in for the last few hours. ØErîright, Iíll just, uh, wait for you out there, okay?Æ Regulus nods, an apology in his mouth that never makes it out. After the curtain closes behind him Dr. Tanner pulls the chair James had been using up to the bed and sits in font of Regulus. ØAlright, well, lets start at the top huh?Æ he says. ØYouíre pretty dehydrated, your iron is concerningly low, so is your blood pressure. But other than that, thereís nothing physically wrong with you that I can see. Certainly nothing that would prevent you from eating.Æ Regulus, of course, knew that. So he doesnít offer up any sort of answer. Hands in his lap, fingers picking at themselves. Eventually the doctor speaks again. ØYou said this has been going on for a long time?Æ He forces himself to speak: ØYeah.Æ ØHave you seen someone about it before?Æ Another Herculean effort: ØYeah,Æ and then, this time sensing he wonít get away with a one word answer: ØI was in an in-patient program at a hospital back home for a few months last year,Æ words bitter. ØAfter that I had a therapist. Prescriptions.Æ Tanner raises his eyebrow. ØWhich prescriptions?Æ He sighs. ØThey started with Lexapro, which helped with the anxiety but not the sleeping. I basically became an insomniac. So they switched me to Zoloft, but that wasnít really doing anything, no matter how they adjusted the doses,Æ he rubs a hand over his face. It had been exhausting, switching between pills, experiencing new and unpleasant side effects, changing doses every few weeks. ØEventually they started me on Paxil, that seemed to work okay.Æ The doctor is watching him, no judgement on his face, or in his voice, when he says: ØThere is a chance the medication might actually be contributing to your problems with food, they do sometimes effect people's appetites.Æ Regulus grimaces. ØNo,Æ and when the doctor continues to sit there patiently, watching him: ØI havenít taken any since August.Æ Tanner nods. ØAt the discretion of your therapist or your GP?Æ ØNeither,Æ he doesnít think he needs to elaborate more than that. There's a short pause, and then: ØWell,Æ Tanner leans forward, elbows on his thighs, paperwork hanging between his knees. ØWe have an eating disorder clinic here at the hospital, they run both in and out patient programsîØ ØNo,Æ Regulus has physically leaned away from the man, but the doctor doesnít seem surprised by this. ØHey kid, youíre eighteen okay? I canít do anything you donít want me to, Iím just letting you know what your options are, and what my opinion as a medical professional is,Æ Regulus continues to eye him warily, ØIíd also recommend you go back on your medication if it was working.Æ Regulus has always found it to be somewhat misleading when people talk about medication working. Itèhelped, sure. But he was never 100% better. Never normal. And every time he had to swallow one of those pills it was like a painful reminder that he was a failure. That he wasnít strong enough to manage on his own. He canít even blame it on his parents because Sirius grew up in the same house and he doesnít need someone to alter his fucking brain chemistry so he can be a normal person. How did I end up here again? He feels tears teasing the edges of his eyes, tickling the back of his throat, and he uses every bit of willpower he has to force them back down. ØAlright, so, hereís what Iím gonna do right now,Æ Tanner goes on, eventually. ØIím gonna hook you up to a bag, get some fluids in you, and Iím gonna bring you a meal replacement shake,Æ Regulus pulls a face and the doctor holds up his hands. ØAgain, I canít force you to do anything, but Iíd really appreciate it if you drank it. Itíll make you feel better, and, between you and me,Æ he leans forward slightly, ØI think itíll make him feel better too,Æ he nods his head towards the curtains through which James disappeared. Regulus wrinkles his nose. ØThey teach you guilt tripping in medical school?Æ The doctor laughs, getting to his feet. ØAbsolutely. You have a flavour preference?Æ ØVanilla,Æ Regulus says instantly. Clean. Pure. He doesnít like shakes. Doesnít like when lots of things are blended up together so that he canít see the individual parts. But if he has to choke one of these down, vanilla will be the easiest. The doctor nods. ØVanilla it is.Æ Regulus and James donít talk on the drive home. Well, at least Regulus doesnít. James tries a few times but when it doesnít get him anywhere he gives up, letting the radio fill the bruised air between them. Regulus sits with his head leaning against the window. Trying to ignore the feeling of the shake in his stomach. Heavy. Greasy. Refusing to be digested. He tries not to imagine it crawling up the walls of his skin. Tries not to be sick. ØReg,Æ James grabs hold of his wrist once they get out of the car. Itís the middle of the night nowîthree in the morning, maybe? Theyíre standing in front of the residence building, and despite Jamesís grip, Regulus resists the impulse to close the space between them. He doesnít say anything but he does look up at James, finally. Does allow him the privilege of having his full attention. Jamesís eyes are searching. Regulus would be surprised if they found anything they were looking for. ØOkay?Æ James asks finally, and with a helpless look on his face. Like heís lost but trying desperately not to show it. Somehow, Regulus manages a weak smile. ØYeah, James. Iím okay. Weíre okay.Æ Lies. Both. Always lying. James smiles back, just as tentatively. ØGood.Æ Evan isnít in their room when Regulus gets back. Or the next morning, when he wakes up. He assumes heís at Cerciís and heís grateful. Regulus doesnít go to the library. Doesnít look at his phone. The only thing he does do is wrap himself in his comforter and hide in the closet. Itís small. And dark. And pathetic. And he tries to imagine that he isnít a person. Tries to imagine that he doesnít exist. Tries to disappear. He has no idea what time it is, when the knocking starts. He registers it vaguely but doesnít make any attempt to move, instead burrowing further into his blankets, squeezing his eyes shut. Happy that whoever it is canít see him. He is so tired of being seen. ØRegulus?Æ Jamesís muffled voice comes through the wood. Regulusís eyes pop open but he doesnít do anything else. ØRegulus come on, open the door.Æ More knocking. ØI checked the library and I know you donít have class right now.Æ Regulus grimaces. It is so like James to memorize his schedule. ØRegîØ but then James cuts himself off. Regulus wonders if itís because someoneís come outside to complain about the noise, or if Evanís come home. But he doesnít hear any voices. And then, a second later, thereís ringing. ØAnswer the phone Reg,Æ James says. ØAnswer the phone or I start yelling.Æ Regulus would really like to avoid a scene, so, begrudgingly, he pulls himself out of the closet and picks up the phone on his desk. ØThank christ,Æ James curses under his breath ØYou fucking scared me, you could have responded to at least one of my texts and let me know you were okay.Æ Regulus hadnít seen the texts, of course. Though that isnít what curdles his stomach. Itís that James had jumped so quickly to fear. For months Regulus has left messages unread, and James has never come running in a panic. Itís just proof that last night changed things in exactly the way Regulus had never wanted them changed. ØRegulus?Æ He sits down on the side of his bed, the phone still pressed to his ear, but he doesnít speak. Canít bear the thought of hearing his own voice. He isnít real. He doesnít exist. Heís disappeared. How can he speak? ØRegulus,Æ James repeats, though something has shifted, his voice quieter, desperate. ØWhat did the doctor say?Æ still whispering. Regulus closes his eyes, letting out a shaking exhale as he curves around the phone like heís just been hit. When several moments pass without a response James sighs on the other end. ØPlease donít do this,Æ another blow. ØPlease donít push me away. You donít have to. Whatever youíre thinking right now, youíre wrong. I just want to take care of you Regulus, please.Æ But thatís just it. Regulus doesnít want to be someone that has to be taken care of. A burden. A liability. He doesnít want James to have to spend his time worrying about him. Sometimes, in his bolder moments, Regulus finds himself dreaming that he could be the one to take care of James. ØOpen the door,Æ Regulus squeezes the phone in his hand so tight heís surprised it doesnít crack. ØCome on, open the door.Æ But he canít. Heís not here. He doesnít exist. Heís disappeared. Thereís a noise in the hallway, Regulus thinks James might be leaning against the door. ØOkay,Æ the other boy says finally. ØOkay, Iíll go. But I justîyou have it all wrong Regulus. You have it all wrong. You think youíre letting us all down, you think youíre failing but youíre not. YouíreîØ and then he laughs wetly. ØItís likeîyou remember the first time we spoke properly? In the library at the start of the year?Æ Regulus nods even though he knows James canít see him, desperately hanging onto every word, snatching them away and hiding them under his bed. Hoarding them like gold. ØYou told me to kill my darlings, right?Æ Regulus has no idea where this is going. ØYou spend all this time trying to be what you think everyone else wantsîyour mom, Sirius, fuck I think you even do it with me. But all those people you think everyone in your life wants you to be, theyíre strangling you. So kill their fucking darlings. Because you, Regulus Black, you are the story.Æ You are the story. ØOkay,Æ voice cracking. ØThatís it. Thatís all. Iím going now. Goodbye Reg.Æ He listens as the line goes dead. And then as Jamesís footsteps fade down the hall. And then he listens to the voices swirling around inside his head: You arenít here. You donít exist. You have disappeared. And then, unexpectedly, hiding just beneath his ribs. In the corner of his heart. At the back of his mind. A voice that heís never heard before whispers: You are the story. Chapter End Notes this whole chapter was written to and sounds like What Was I Made For by Billie Eilish ANYWAY the next chapter is another Regulus / Jegulus centric chapter and then we're gonna get more Wolfstar content and then ***BARTY*** SO many things to look forward to! i hope the chapter was okay and not like boring . . . it was written in transit so who knows! i think i'm going to try and focus on kyd since there aren't many chapters left and just pump them out more consistently so the next chapter should be coming at you soooooooonnnnnn! Chapter25 Chapter Summary You're a fucking idiot Chapter Notes tw reference to self-harm tw ED cw mild violence See the end of the chapter for more notes Part I Regulus One Year Earlier ØSENIOR YEAR BITCH!Æ Cerci shouts, as she sits across from Regulus at one of the picnic benches in front of their school. He doesnít look up from his textbook. ØYou canít say that everyday.Æ ØIs that a bet?Æ He can hear the grin in her voice. ØNo.Æ ØCímon, how much do you want to bet I canít say it everyday?Æ ØZero dollars.Æ ØCause Iíll take that action.Æ ØThere is no action.Æ ØTwenty dollars? Fifty?Æ Regulus sighs, finally looking up only to find her smiling triumphantly back at him, clearly pleased that her goading has succeeded in getting his attention. ØGood morning Regulus,Æ she says obnoxiously. ØI see you are in a fabulous mood as always today.Æ He rolls his eyes. ØGood morning, Cerci.Æ As if on cue the bell rings, causing the loitering students standing in huddles across the lawn to start meandering towards the doors. Cerci slips her arm through Regulusís the second heís on his feet. Heíd shove her off but he knows itís no use. ØI have a feeling this is going to be a good day,Æ she says, practically skipping at his side. Regulus sighs. ØYou always have that feeling.Æ ØDoesnít make it any less true!Æ she practically sings. ØIf you say so.Æ ØI do.Æ ØAlright.Æ ØFine.Æ ØGreat.Æ Cerci leans in just a little bit closer, big eyes staring at him imploringly even though Regulus keeps his gaze forward, doing his best not to encourage her. ØSay it, go on.Æ ØNo.Æ ØOh come on Regulus,Æ she pulls on the arm sheís currently clutching. ØSay it, I know you wanna!Æ ØI promise you, I donít.Æ But she makes big puppy dog eyes at him, dragging her feet in a way that will almost certainly make them late for class. After several seconds of this Regulus finally gives in, looking at her properly. ØThis is going to be a good day,Æ he says flatly. Cerci throws her free arm in the air. ØHell yeah it is!Æ and then, just when Regulus thinks the worst of it is over, she tilts her head towards the sky and shouts: ØSenior year bitch!!!Æ ØChrist,Æ Regulus mutters, embarrassed, but Cerci only laughs, the noise trailing after them as they enter the building. It was not, unfortunately, a good day. Regulus knows something is wrong the second he gets home from school. His motherís car is in the driveway for one thing, and sheís rarely ever home before five pm. Regulus lets his knapsack slide off his shoulder and onto the foyer floor, anxiety beginning to itch at the inside of his skin. ØHello?Æ he calls out, taking a hesitant step forward. His voice echoes, the house feeling somehow emptier and colder than it had when he left that morning. ØWeíre upstairs,Æ comes the returning call, Regulusís eyes jumping to the staircase. Weíre? Both his parents are home? For a moment he just stands there, eyes staring above him like heíll suddenly develop x-ray vision and be able to see what his parents are doing. He runs through the list of reasons they might be waiting for himîare they getting a divorce? did the school call?îheís pretty sure his grades are okay. And by pretty sure he means he knows. Knows for a fact they are. Itís senior year. His whole life has become nothing but an obsession with grades. Tests. Assignments. His mother hovering over his shoulder as he fills out university applications. Making her disapproval clear anytime he applies somewhere out of town. She dictates the courses to him, the programs sheíll accept. She reads over his essays, tells him what heís doing wrong, where he falls short. ØRegulus! Come up here please!Æ Her voice jolts him back to the present, and he forces himself to uncurl his fists as he starts up the stairs. Heíd been clutching them so tightly he can feel the imprints of his nails in his palms. He falters again when he gets to the top of the stairs, when he sees which door is open, which room theyíre in. He very seriously considers turning around and running back out the front door. The itching has turned to claws. The anxiety a beast inside him. Destroying his insides. Shredding them up. Heís shaking something fierce as he starts to step towards his room. Somehow he already knows what heíll find and yet heís still unprepared for it. His mother is standing by his bed, arms crossed over her chest, face stern. His father sitting in his desk chair, elbows on his knees, head lifting when Regulus walks into the room. He freezes just past the doorway, body going cold. His bed is covered in food. So is his floor. Boxes of cereal, of crackers, of pasta. Bags of chips. And then thereís other food, some wrapped in napkins, some moldy beyond recognition. Itís been sitting beneath his bed, rotting. ØWell?Æ Walburga demands. ØCare to explain yourself?Æ No. No. No. No. ØIîØ ØWhat the hell is this Regulus?Æ she jabs her finger accusingly at the bed before he can finish. Regulus flinches, his mouth snapping shut. This house is big, yet suddenly the walls are closing in. ØBecause it looks like something a crazy person would do.Æ ØWalburga,Æ his father says softly. ØWell?Æ she demands. ØDoesnít it? I mean, Christ Regulus, what do you expect us to think about this?Æ He isnít sure when it started. Just that the more stress he felt the less he could eat, but the more obsessed with food he became. He wanted to see it. Touch it. Line it up in a row. He couldnít eat it. Could never eat it. But there was something satisfying about placing it in colour coded piles. Sometimes he would pull it out from under his bed or his desk drawers and look at it. Count it. Organize it all for the hundredth time. ØRegulus?Æ his mother snaps her fingers. ØHello?Æ He is flooded with shame. At being exposed like this. At being seen. ØItís nothing. Itís nothing. Whatîwhat are you even doing in here?Æ ØThe maids were complaining about the smell.Æ Ah. The smell. It had crept up so gradually, a sort of musk in the air, a heaviness. Rotting food. The truth is, Regulus kind of likes it. It tickles some weird part of him. Itís the same feeling he gets when he breaks the food down. Crushing it in its packages. Squishing it. Ruining it. He isnít sure what thatís about. Tries not to think about it too much. Ends up thinking about it all the time. For someone who barely eats, his world revolves around food. When he isnít thinking about homework, about university applications, about his future. Itís food, food, food, food. ØGet out,Æ he finally manages, trembling slightly. ØAnd what? Leave this mess here to rot?Æ ØGet. Out.Æ She doesnít listen, of course. Maybe she doesnít even hear him. Regulus has long suspected that to be the case. Even when theyíre in the same room, even when heís screaming, she never hears him. Øîour food, food weíve paid for. Just going to waste!Æ He is ashamed. And he is angry. And he is barely himself. There is something in him and it is twisted and wrong and fucked up and loud. So loud. And he just needs to be left alone. He just needs to be able to put it all back in order. He just needs to be able to crush it between his fingers. He just needs to feel that familiar ache that means heís hungry. He needs to sit with it. And hurt and hurt and feel like he deserves it. And feel like heís doing so good when he doesnít give in. When he survives it. Needs to feel like heís finally doing so good. Finally got control. He just needs them out of here. He just needs them to stop looking at him. ØRegulusîØ ØGet out, get out, GET OUT!Æ His father is standing now, arms outstretched, pleading. ØRegulus, calm down.Æ ØGet out of my room!Æ he sounds like heís five. He feels like heís five. Or eleven, his brother gone, leaving him all alone in this empty fucking house. If youíd been better, whispers the voice in his head, if youíd been better he wouldnít have left. Mom wouldnít have yelled. They wouldnít all hate coming home. It isnít the food thatís rotten. It isnít the food that smells. You are spoiled. You are bruised. You are nothing that anyone wants. ØGet out of my room!Æ ØRegulus, stop shouting,Æ his mother admonishes, though she doesnít approach him, doesnít try to touch him. He doesnít blame her. ØJust breathe okay?Æ his father is saying. ØJust take a deep breath and weíll talk about this.Æ ØYouíre acting like an insane person,Æ Walburga says, mostly under her breath. Regulus is. He is insane. He is crazy. He knows that. He knows. He just canít stop. Canít make it stop. Doesnít want to. Canít see the point. Heís glad they still donít know about the marks on the insides of his arms. His thighs. It isnít that Regulus wants to die. That would be too simple. Too bold. Regulus doesnít want to die. He just wants to have never been born. He thinks his mother probably wants that too. She moves towards the bed then, sheís speaking, he can see her mouth moving, but all he hears is static. Is his own heartbeat, pounding against his eardrum. She starts to clear up the food on his bed. Sheís mixing it up. Sheís touching it. Sheís taking it away. ØStop,Æ he says, stepping forward, but his father is there, blocking him. Heís speaking too but Regulus canít understand him. Itís like he can hear his voice but itís all just sound. Not even words. ØStop,Æ he says again, straining against his fatherís grip, trying to get to his mother. ØStop. Stop it. Get out. Get out! Leave me alone! Get out!Æ She throws something in the trashcan by his desk, he doesnít even know what, and that sets off a new wave of panic in him. He shoves his father hard, the older man stumbling but still managing to grab Regulusís arm, pulling him back. Yanking himîprobably more violently than he would have if he werenít off balance. And Regulus isnít thinking. He isnít himself. So when heís pulled back he turns around and punches him. Walburga screams, Orionís hand going to his face as blood gushes down from his nose, eyes wide, staring at Regulus like heís scared of him. He probably is. A sick feeling rocks through Regulus as he holds his arms up, Walburga still shrieking as he backs himself all the way against the far wall. ØIím sorry,Æ he says, voice cracking in horror. ØIím sorry. Iím sorry. Fuck, Iím sorry. I didnít meanîI shouldnít haveîIím sorry.Æ His mother goes to his father, and Regulus sinks to the floor, curling himself up as tightly as he can. Knees pressing into his chest, arms wrapping around his legs. It doesnít stop the shaking. You are spoiled. You are bruised. You are nothing that anyone wants. A few days later heís in a hospital. Clothes stripped from him, hospital gown on. They run blood tests, urine tests, drug tests. He stands on a scale, they pinch the skin at his hip, place cold stethoscopes against his abdomen, his heart, shuffling him from one room to the next. Regulus feels numb, which he imagines is for the best. People talk to him but they donít say very much. They do find the cuts. The pink puckering lines beginning to heal. The ugly scabbed ones heís just torn open. They say more things. Regulus doesnít say much back. Eventually they sit him down in a room with his parents. Heís allowed the dignity of putting his clothes back on. Thereís a doctor, a psychologist, a nutritionistîa whole team. Regulus fidgets on the couch, feeling very much like heís on trial. More words. Words, words, words. He isnít following really. They talk about medication. About therapy. About food. ØWe believe Regulus has disordered eatingÆ and ØWe would recommend he enter our in.patient program.Æ His mother wrinkles her nose. ØAn eating disorder,Æ she says with distaste, turning to him. ØReally Regulus? I thought you were better than this.Æ Regulus doesnít say anything. He spends the next three months in the hospital. Confined to a wheelchair because they wonít let him walk. Donít want him burning calories. Not that exercise has ever been Regulusís problem, but they donít seem to care. His life becomes sterile halls, and fluorescent lights, and a rotation of nurses and doctors and examinations that never seem to end. Senior year bitch. Sitting in his dorm room closet almost a year latter, surrounded by blankets, he still canít get his motherís words out of his head. I thought you were better than this. I thought you were better than this. I thought you were better. Yeah, he thinks, resigned. I did too. Part II James He goes straight to Siriusís, barely able to think the whole way there, a cold sort of dread descending on him during the drive. Itís the way he feels after a bad hit on the ice, when heís lying there, wind knocked out of him, wondering whether or not heís going to be able to move ever again. The panic before the dust settles and he knows how bad it really is. When all the worst things are still possible. Thatís how he feels right now. He doesnít remember parking his car. Isnít sure what time it is. He probably should have called Sirius, checked that he was home, but he hadnít been thinking things through. Hadnít been thinking at all really. Heís on a weird sort of autopilot. Just moving. His hands shaking when he starts knocking on the apartment door. ØSirius!Æ he calls out, fear climbing up his voice. ØSirius!Æ Please, he thinks, please be home. Please, please, please. ØSiîØ The door swings open and Sirius is there; baggy shirt, gym shorts, hair tied backîfamiliar. Safe. Home. James feels himself exhale. Somehow, despite everything, the sight of Sirius settles something in James. You and me. Itís you and me. ØJames?Æ Sirius asks, brow furrowed, concern creasing his eyes. ØWhat is it? ØYeah Mac?Æ ØTake off his shirt.Æ Thereís laughter. ØWhat?Æ James demands. ØNo fucking way, leave him alone,Æ Sirius shoves Mulciber back, only to be grabbed from behind by Benny and put in a headlock. ØWhat the hell!Æ But before James can even take a step he has Macís hand planted firmly in the centre of his chest, shoving him into the wall. ØJesus,Æ Mac says, laughing while he shakes his head. ØYou rookies really need to learn your place.Æ ØWe need to get him help,Æ James tries and fails to shove Mac back. The other boy is older and stronger. ØHeís fine dude, I told you, this always happens. You need to chill.Æ Over Macís shoulder James can see Sirius, still restrained, Peterís shirt is off, he has no idea where itís gone. Benny must have handed off the marker because thereís someone else drawing on Peterís chest. Or maybe thereís more than one marker. Maybe they planned this. Tradition. Itís weird, because around them the party just keeps going. Music and keg stands and beer pong. Like this is normal. Like James is overreacting. Maybe he is.
